The painter, a narrow man with tobacco-stained fingers, had been summoned in haste and told only that the work was to be done quickly, accurately, and without questions. He agreed to two of those terms. By the second sitting he had already decided that accuracy was the dangerous part.

The young woman who posed for him wore black lace at her throat and dark velvet at her wrists. Her posture never faltered. Her expression changed only in degrees too small to measure, yet the room altered whenever she lifted her eyes from her folded hands. On the first day, the painter blamed fatigue for the strange pull of her face. On the second, he blamed the weather. On the third, while mixing shadow into the curve beneath one painted eye, he felt a certainty settle over him with the quiet weight of a hand on the shoulder: he was not painting a likeness for the present, but for someone who had not yet arrived.

When the portrait was finished, the mistress of the house stood before it for a long time without speaking. Then she ordered it hung in the west corridor and instructed the staff that it was never to be removed, never to be covered, and never to be discussed with guests. Years later, after names had faded from ledgers and whole branches of the family had thinned into rumor, the house remained faithful to that instruction. Some promises, once made inside Harrowick House, did not weaken with time. They waited.

parts.title: Chapter One: Byline After Midnight
paragraphs: Anna Karstane told herself she’d agreed to this for the story. That was the clean version she could say out loud: a first-person overnight in a “haunted” estate, written with enough skepticism to annoy the campus occult club and enough atmosphere to make the arts editor smile. The messier truth sat underneath it—her curiosity had teeth, and she’d always had the habit of touching things that warned her away.

The taxi’s heater worked in short bursts, like it was negotiating with the cold. Outside, rain stitched the night into something dense and private. The driver didn’t talk much, which Anna appreciated; she was already drafting the opening paragraph in her head and deleting it at the same time.

She looked like she belonged to the edge of a concert poster rather than a journalism department: black jeans, combat boots, a fitted leather jacket that smelled faintly of smoke and old perfume. A silver choker caught the occasional dashboard glow. Her eyeliner, applied with the confidence of practice, had started to soften at one corner where rain had found its way in—an imperfect smear that made her look tired and defiant at once.

In her bag were the tools of a student reporter from the late eighties: a notepad with dog-eared pages, a cheap film camera she’d borrowed from the media lab, and a small cassette recorder she used to dictate quotes so she wouldn’t lose them to adrenaline. She didn’t call it ghost-hunting. She called it documentation.

Her editor at the student paper had made a show of being responsible. *Don’t sensationalize,* he’d said, pushing his glasses up. *And don’t sue us.* Then he’d leaned closer and added, *If you come back with something good, I’ll put it above the sports column for once.*

Harrowick House sat on the edge of a town that pretended it didn’t have a history. The older locals didn’t talk about the manor so much as they talked around it, the way people skirted potholes they’d hit before. A cracked sign leaned in ivy: HARROWICK HOUSE. The taxi turned onto the gravel lane, and the tires made a sound like someone quietly grinding their teeth.

The estate appeared through the rain in pieces—gables, dark windows, a tower that held on to the sky. One upper window glowed with a dull warmth, a single square that looked almost domestic until you noticed how still it was. Anna took that in and filed it away. Lights had explanations. If she couldn’t find one, she’d write that down too.

A man waited under the portico, coat collar up, posture tight. Mr. Harrowick matched the voice from the phone: careful, controlled, the kind of person who measured his own words as if they cost money. He didn’t offer a dramatic greeting. He offered a keyring and a look that asked her to be competent.
“You’re Anna Karstane,” he said. He pronounced her name correctly, which surprised her.
“That’s me.” She tried a smile that came out more like a baring of teeth. “Student journalist. I’ll do my best to disappoint everyone who wants this to be a séance.”

Harrowick’s mouth twitched, a quick movement he didn’t let become a smile. “I don’t want theatrics,” he said. “I want someone who observes.” His gaze went to the recorder in her hand. “You’re recording?”
“Notes,” Anna said. “Quotes. Details. It keeps me honest.”

He nodded once. “Good. Then here are the terms. You stay inside until dawn. You don’t bring anyone in, and you don’t wander the grounds at night. If you decide to leave, you leave a note on the hall table. Clear and obvious. No riddles.”
“And in the morning?” Anna asked.

“The taxi returns at seven,” Harrowick said. “One knock at the door. If you answer, you leave. If you don’t, the driver waits ten minutes, then goes back to town and calls me. I return with a spare key.” His jaw tightened on the next part. “If I still can’t reach you by nine, I call emergency services. I don’t want attention,” he added, voice flattening, “and I want you alive more.”
Anna raised her brows. “That’s almost reassuring.”

“It’s practical,” Harrowick said. He hesitated, then looked past her shoulder as if the house were standing behind her like a person. “There’s one more thing. If you find… items. Letters, photographs, anything you think is related. Leave them where they are.”
“Because the house doesn’t like it?” Anna asked, aiming for lightness.
Harrowick’s eyes stayed dry. “Because I don’t trust what changes when things are moved,” he said. “And because I’m tired of being wrong about this place.”

The driver cleared his throat. Anna glanced back at the taxi, then at Harrowick. Alone meant alone; her editor had hated that part, but it made the feature cleaner. A witness who could be quoted, a setting that could do its work without an audience.

Harrowick stepped into the taxi’s back seat. The driver pulled away. Headlights swept across the front steps, made the wet stone shine, and then the lane swallowed the light. The engine noise thinned into rain and finally disappeared.

Silence took its place with unsettling speed. Anna stood under the portico and listened. Her pulse felt loud in her ears, and she resented it on principle. She dug out a cigarette, lit it with her back to the wind, and drew smoke into her lungs like it was punctuation.

She exhaled slowly. “It’s just a building,” she told herself, because saying it anchored her. The cigarette burned down faster than she expected. She crushed it under her boot and pocketed the pack again.

The front door opened with a weighty sigh of hinges. Inside, the air smelled of dry wood and something faintly floral, like old sachets. Anna stepped over the threshold and felt the temperature change against her skin, a coolness that didn’t belong to the season.

For a heartbeat, her perception did something odd. The hall seemed to tilt. She had the distinct sensation of hovering an inch behind herself, watching her own shoulders rise and fall under the leather jacket, watching her hand tighten around the keyring. The feeling snapped back so quickly it left a thin nausea behind.

Anna blinked, swallowed, and blamed adrenaline. She’d pulled all-nighters in the newsroom; her body did strange things when it wanted sleep and didn’t get it. That explanation tasted sensible, so she kept it.

The entrance hall rose high enough to swallow sound. A staircase climbed at the far end. Lamps cast weak pools of light through yellowed shades, and portraits covered the walls: severe men, pale women, children dressed like small adults. Their painted eyes didn’t follow her. They didn’t need to.

Anna picked a narrow table in the center of the hall and set up with the kind of deliberate neatness she pretended she didn’t possess. Notebook. Camera. Recorder. Flashlight. She liked order; it made the night feel like a series of tasks rather than a dare.

She pressed the recorder’s button and spoke softly, the way she did when she wanted her future self to trust her past self. “Entry one. Harrowick House. Friday night. Rain. Arrived twenty-two forty-seven.” She paused, listened to the room, then added, almost amused, “And yes, I’m alone. If you’re hearing this and I sound braver than I feel, that’s editing.”

She walked, keeping her dictation going in short bursts—drawing room, covered furniture, a faint smell of dust and fabric; library, rows of books that made her feel like an intruder in someone else’s private mind. She noted the mundane possibilities automatically: drafts, settling beams, animals in walls, old pipes expanding and contracting.

The house offered small interruptions. A cooler patch of air that moved against her wrist when there were no open windows. A subtle shift in pressure near the west corridor that made her ears feel full. Anna caught herself slowing, her body responding before her skepticism could organize a sentence.

She reached the corridor and felt something tighten behind her ribs. The portraits here were closer together, more intimate, less formal. Some of the faces looked unfinished around the edges, as if the painter had been interrupted and never returned.

Halfway down, one painting hung slightly lower than the others, the frame’s angle off by a few degrees. Anna stopped because her eyes stopped. The corridor seemed to narrow toward that frame, the house’s quiet arranging itself into a point.
She had the sense that her next breath mattered. She let it out anyway and stepped closer.

The portrait showed a young woman in nineteenth-century dress—dark lace at the throat, hair pinned in heavy curls. The painter had given her an expression that held both patience and amusement, as if she were waiting for someone to catch up.
Anna’s mouth dried.
The face was hers.

Not in the loose, flattering way people claimed strangers resembled each other. The same cheekbones. The same small asymmetry in the mouth. The same faint shadow beneath the left eye that makeup never fully erased. Even the tiny scar near her hairline, earned in childhood and usually hidden by her bangs, sat there on the painted skin like a signature.
She leaned in until her breath fogged the varnish. A date sat in the corner: **1894**.

Anna stepped back, recorder still running in her pocket, and tried to manufacture an explanation. Prank. Coincidence. Some creepy art student with too much time. Her mind offered options like flashcards, all of them flimsy.
A floorboard creaked upstairs.

Anna froze, then forced herself to move. She walked back to the hall table with the stiff carefulness of someone pretending she isn’t listening. She stopped the recorder, ejected the cassette, and rewound it, the mechanism’s whirr giving her something solid to hold on to.
She pressed play.
At first, the tape gave her rain, her own footsteps, the calmness she’d performed into the microphone. Then came a quiet exhale, so close it raised gooseflesh along her arms.
Anna held the recorder tighter.
A whisper surfaced under the breath, thin and structured, as if the sound had been threaded through static on purpose.
“An…na.”
Her name, pulled apart and put back together by something that didn’t use it often.
The tape clicked. Silence returned.
From the bottom of the staircase, something shifted into the edge of the lamplight—slow, deliberate—claiming the space where her skepticism had been standing a moment before.

chapters.title: Chapter Two: The House Takes Dictation
paragraphs: Anna stood very still and watched the lamplight by the staircase, waiting for her eyes to decide what they were seeing. The shape at the edge of the glow had moved with intention—she felt that much in her bones—even if her brain wanted to file it under drafts, shadows, and tiredness.

She swallowed and forced herself to breathe like a person doing a job. A reporter didn’t get to panic on schedule. She clicked the recorder off, then on again, and spoke into it in a low voice she hoped sounded steadier than she felt. “Entry two. Playback captured a whisper. It sounded like my name.” She paused, then added, “I’m going to assume this is acoustics until it stops being acoustics.”
The hall held its breath with her.

The shadow by the stairs shifted again—small, slow, as though something had leaned away from the light. Anna’s shoulders tightened. She reached for the flashlight on the table and snapped it on, sweeping the beam across the base of the staircase.

A coat stand stood there, thin and old, its hooks raised like patient hands. No coat. No hat. Just wood worn shiny by a century of fingertips. The lamplight had turned it into a person at the edge of vision, and now, under the flashlight, it looked harmlessly stupid.

Anna let out air through her teeth and felt anger arrive on the heels of relief. “Cute,” she muttered, and hated how the word sounded in the empty house. She circled the stand, checked the floorboards around it, then looked back at the lamp. The shade hung slightly crooked. A hinge somewhere up the stairs complained softly, a single creak like a throat clearing.

She set the flashlight down and rubbed at the corner of her eye. The wet smear of eyeliner came away on her finger, making her look less like a composed cynic and more like a student who’d made a long night personal. She wiped her hand on her jeans and reached for her cigarette pack.

Smoke helped her think in straight lines. She lit up and leaned one hip against the hall table, eyes on the staircase while she inhaled. The lighter’s flame had been the brightest thing in the house since the taxi left. That fact bothered her more than it should have.

She tried the simplest test first: she walked to the front door and checked the lock. Solid. No gap. No draft. She checked the nearest windows. Shut. Latched. The air was still. The temperature felt even, cool but steady.

Anna’s skepticism was a muscle; she’d trained it for years. It had gotten her through professors who loved their own voices and sources who loved a story more than the truth. It worked best when she fed it small tasks.
So she gave herself tasks.

She returned to the staircase, climbed three steps, and stopped. The wood groaned under her weight like an old man waking up. She looked down at the hall from above, at the table where her gear sat in its tidy lineup. Camera. Notebook. Recorder. Flashlight. The little island of control she’d built in a room that didn’t want islands.

A brief dizziness slid through her, the same wrong lightness she’d felt when she first entered. The hall seemed to fall away from her, as though her viewpoint had floated upward and backward, hovering near the ceiling beam. For a second she watched herself from an impossible angle: Anna on the stairs, cigarette ember glowing, hair damp at the ends, one hand gripping the banister as if it could keep her anchored.
Her stomach rolled. She blinked hard and the world snapped back into its usual geometry.
“Stress,” she whispered into the recorder without meaning to, then stopped herself. She didn’t want the tape full of excuses. She wanted it full of facts.

She climbed down again and walked toward the west corridor with the stiff determination of someone refusing to be steered. The portraits watched with their practiced stillness. Their faces belonged to people who had died with secrets in their mouths, and the house had kept those secrets like furniture.

The corridor felt narrower than it had earlier, as though the air itself had thickened. Anna’s skin prickled along her arms. She hated the way her body behaved, offering reactions before thoughts. That belonged to superstition, to the kind of story she’d promised her editor she wouldn’t write.

The portrait waited where she’d left it, hung a fraction too low, frame tilted like a question. Anna kept her distance this time. She took out the camera, advanced the film with a careful click, and took a photo straight-on. The flash fired, harsh and white, collapsing the corridor into a flat image for a heartbeat. When the afterimage faded, the painted woman’s eyes seemed even darker.
Anna wrote in her notebook with a fast hand: *Portrait resembles subject. Date 1894. Photograph taken 23:12.* The act of writing steadied her.

She leaned closer again despite herself, eyes scanning the brushwork for signs of modern tampering. The paint looked old. The cracks in the varnish formed a delicate web across the surface. Her reflection hovered faintly in the gloss: leather jacket, pale face, smudged eyeliner, lips pressed tight.

For a moment, the reflection and the painted face lined up too perfectly, and Anna felt a surge of irritation at her own imagination. She stepped back, breaking the alignment, and the corridor’s temperature seemed to ease by a degree.
Footsteps sounded above her.

Anna turned fast enough to make her necklace bite her throat. The sound came again—one creak, then another—slow and measured. It traveled along the upstairs landing and stopped. Silence followed, thick enough to feel against the ears.
She held the recorder in her palm and kept it rolling without speaking. If this house wanted to perform, she’d let it perform into evidence.
Nothing else moved. No rush of air. No slam of a door. The quiet sat there with an almost polite patience.

Anna returned to the hall and checked the old phone line Harrowick had mentioned. The handset felt heavy, the plastic faintly sticky as if it had absorbed decades of fingerprints. She lifted it to her ear.
No dial tone. Not even a faint hum. Just dead, clean silence.

She put it down carefully and stared at the staircase again. Her cigarette had burned low. She crushed it out and didn’t light another; she refused to let the night turn into a chain of small dependencies.

She sat at the table and rewound the tape she’d recorded earlier. The mechanical whirr made her feel less alone. She pressed play and listened with her head tilted slightly, as if angle could improve reception.
Rain. Her own footsteps. The small rasp of her breath. Then the exhale again—close, intimate, impossible to mistake for a creaking floorboard.
Her fingers tightened around the recorder.
Beneath that breath, the whisper returned with more shape than before. The syllables came gently, spaced as if whoever spoke them was practicing.
“An…na.”
The name sounded familiar and wrong at the same time, like hearing your nickname from someone who never had the right to use it.

Anna stopped the tape and stared at the recorder’s little window, at the spinning reels that had already gone still. She didn’t feel triumphant. She didn’t feel terrified. She felt insulted, as though the house had reached into her pocket and touched something private.

She made herself do the boring checks again because boring checks were how you kept your mind from running off and writing its own headlines. She inspected the recorder’s microphone port for dust. She checked the cassette for defects. She rewound and played the same section twice more.
The whisper held. Every time. Same breath, same soft thread of sound, same careful spacing of her name.
Anna’s skepticism didn’t vanish. It shifted into a new, narrower shape: *How.* If something could be measured, it could be explained, even if the explanation arrived later.

She stood, slid the cassette into her jacket pocket, and picked up the flashlight. She didn’t aim it at the stairs right away. She aimed it at the portraits lining the hall, letting the beam pass over faces and names and frames. Brass plaques glinted. Dates. Titles. A family tree in oil paint.

One frame near the base of the staircase caught the light and threw it back. The glass over the portrait had been recently cleaned, too clean compared to the others. Anna leaned in to read the plaque and found her own reflection staring back at her in the shine.
Behind her reflection, in the dark curve of the staircase, a second figure seemed to stand for half a second—tall, narrow, placed one step higher than she was.
Anna turned, flashlight swinging.
The staircase was empty.

Her heart kicked hard enough to make her dizzy. For an instant the world loosened again, that out-of-step sensation, as if her mind had lifted out of her skull to watch her from above. She clamped a hand on the banister and forced the feeling down through her feet, through the wood, through the only solid thing in reach.
A sound rose from upstairs—soft fabric moving, the kind of noise a person makes when they shift their weight and try to stay quiet.
Anna stared up the staircase, breath shallow, recorder in her pocket like a small captured secret.
Then, from the landing, came a single, deliberate tap—as if someone had touched the railing with a fingernail to make sure she was listening.

scenes.title: Why She Came
scenes.summary: Introduces Anna's stated reason for the overnight and hints at her deeper curiosity. Establishes her voice as a skeptical student journalist prepared to probe the house. Sets up the personal risk she enjoys despite claiming it is for a story.

scenes.title: Tools and Briefing
scenes.summary: Lists Anna's reporter kit and recalls the editor's instructions to avoid sensationalism. The scene shifts outward as the taxi approaches the estate and the setting is named. Prepares the reader for arrival and the practical framing of the assignment.

scenes.title: Harrowick Appears
scenes.summary: The house arrives in view through the rain and a figure waits under the portico. Mr. Harrowick is introduced physically and in tone, establishing a formal, controlled presence. Anna notes a domestic-looking light and files it away as a possible mundane explanation.

scenes.title: First Exchange
scenes.summary: Anna and Harrowick trade brief, measured dialogue as they meet at the door. His guarded manner and her reporter posture are shown in close interaction. The recorder becomes a visible point of focus.

scenes.title: Record and Rules
scenes.summary: Anna identifies her recording methods and Harrowick lays out strict terms for her stay. The conditions emphasize isolation and observable procedures. This frames the logistical boundaries for the overnight.

scenes.title: Morning Procedure
scenes.summary: Anna questions what happens in the morning and Harrowick explains the pickup routine and safety measures. He stresses practical steps and expresses a personal concern about her wellbeing. Anna reacts with mild surprise at the seriousness of the plan.

scenes.title: Leave Things Be
scenes.summary: Harrowick adds a rule about not moving items and gives a terse explanation that points to past mistakes. Anna tests the restraint with a lighthearted question. Harrowick's weary insistence sketches his relationship to the house.

scenes.title: Departure
scenes.summary: The driver clears his throat and Harrowick leaves in the taxi, ending the immediate human presence. The engine departs and the house is left to Anna alone. This closes the handoff and isolates her in the setting.

scenes.title: Under the Portico
scenes.summary: Anna stands under the portico in sudden silence and smokes to steady herself. She repeats a grounding line to maintain composure. This scene captures the brief moment between public departure and entering the house.

scenes.title: Threshold Sensation
scenes.summary: The front door opens and Anna steps into an interior smelling of old wood and sachets. She experiences a disorienting sensation of watching herself from behind, which she tries to attribute to fatigue. The house's ambient coolness registers as odd.

scenes.title: Rationalizing the Odd
scenes.summary: Anna blames adrenaline for the strange feeling and moves forward. The entrance hall is described—high ceiling, lamps, portraits—establishing the house's atmosphere and the portraits as elements to watch. The scene transitions into preparation for work.

scenes.title: Setting Up
scenes.summary: Anna methodically arranges her notebook, camera, recorder, and flashlight at a hall table. She begins an audio entry to document the night, outlining time and conditions. She then walks and catalogs rooms, noting ordinary explanations for sounds and smells.

scenes.title: Small Interruptions
scenes.summary: Anna notices subtle physical phenomena—a cool patch of air and a pressure shift—that slow her skepticism. She reaches a corridor where portraiture becomes more intimate and unfinished, changing the mood. The focus narrows toward a particular painting.

scenes.title: Pulled Toward a Frame
scenes.summary: The corridor narrows toward a slightly askew portrait and Anna feels the moment matter. She steps closer despite the sensation and reads the painting's details. The portrait itself is described, setting up the recognition.

scenes.title: Uncanny Resemblance
scenes.summary: Anna realizes the painted woman's face matches her own in specific, personal ways. The narrative lists mirrored features and a childhood scar that solidify the resemblance. She inspects the painting and notices a date.

scenes.title: Trying Explanations
scenes.summary: Anna recoils and searches for mundane explanations like pranks or coincidence. That moment is interrupted by a creak from upstairs, returning attention to the present danger. The scene pivots from internal rationale back to immediate auditory cues.

scenes.title: Playback
scenes.summary: She freezes, then moves deliberately to the hall table to stop and rewind the recorder. She presses play to check whether what she heard was captured. This sequence shifts focus to recorded evidence as a stabilizing object.

scenes.title: A Breath on Tape
scenes.summary: The playback yields the ambient rain and her footsteps, then a close, quiet exhale. Anna reacts physically by gripping the recorder tighter, indicating rising alarm. The tape becomes proof that something else is present.

scenes.title: Whisper and Motion
scenes.summary: A whisper articulates her name on the tape and then silence follows. Immediately after, something moves into the lamplight at the base of the staircase, shifting the scene from audio evidence to visible presence. The chapter closes on this threatened encounter.

# The Portrait at Harrowick House

In the autumn of 1894, Harrowick House kept its lights burning later than usual. Rain worried at the windows, and the servants moved through the corridors with lowered voices, carrying coal, lamps, and trays they hoped would not be sent back untouched. Something in the household had shifted during the previous month. No one named it. The house did not encourage that kind of honesty.

The portrait was begun on a Thursday in the west corridor, where the light held longest and the walls kept a little warmth after sunset. The painter, a narrow man with tobacco-stained fingers, had been summoned in haste and told only that the work was to be done quickly, accurately, and without questions. He agreed to two of those terms. By the second sitting he had already decided that accuracy was the dangerous part.

The young woman who posed for him wore black lace at her throat and dark velvet at her wrists. Her posture never faltered. Her expression changed only in degrees too small to measure, yet the room altered whenever she lifted her eyes from her folded hands. On the first day, the painter blamed fatigue for the strange pull of her face. On the second, he blamed the weather. On the third, while mixing shadow into the curve beneath one painted eye, he felt a certainty settle over him with the quiet weight of a hand on the shoulder: he was not painting a likeness for the present, but for someone who had not yet arrived.

When the portrait was finished, the mistress of the house stood before it for a long time without speaking. Then she ordered it hung in the west corridor and instructed the staff that it was never to be removed, never to be covered, and never to be discussed with guests. Years later, after names had faded from ledgers and whole branches of the family had thinned into rumor, the house remained faithful to that instruction. Some promises, once made inside Harrowick House, did not weaken with time. They waited.

## Chapter One: Byline After Midnight

Anna Karstane told herself she’d agreed to this for the story. That was the clean version she could say out loud: a first-person overnight in a “haunted” estate, written with enough skepticism to annoy the campus occult club and enough atmosphere to make the arts editor smile. The messier truth sat underneath it—her curiosity had teeth, and she’d always had the habit of touching things that warned her away.

The taxi’s heater worked in short bursts, like it was negotiating with the cold. Outside, rain stitched the night into something dense and private. The driver didn’t talk much, which Anna appreciated; she was already drafting the opening paragraph in her head and deleting it at the same time.

She looked like she belonged to the edge of a concert poster rather than a journalism department: black jeans, combat boots, a fitted leather jacket that smelled faintly of smoke and old perfume. A silver choker caught the occasional dashboard glow. Her eyeliner, applied with the confidence of practice, had started to soften at one corner where rain had found its way in—an imperfect smear that made her look tired and defiant at once.

In her bag were the tools of a student reporter from the late eighties: a notepad with dog-eared pages, a cheap film camera she’d borrowed from the media lab, and a small cassette recorder she used to dictate quotes so she wouldn’t lose them to adrenaline. She didn’t call it ghost-hunting. She called it documentation.

Her editor at the student paper had made a show of being responsible. *Don’t sensationalize,* he’d said, pushing his glasses up. *And don’t sue us.* Then he’d leaned closer and added, *If you come back with something good, I’ll put it above the sports column for once.*

Harrowick House sat on the edge of a town that pretended it didn’t have a history. The older locals didn’t talk about the manor so much as they talked around it, the way people skirted potholes they’d hit before. A cracked sign leaned in ivy: HARROWICK HOUSE. The taxi turned onto the gravel lane, and the tires made a sound like someone quietly grinding their teeth.

The estate appeared through the rain in pieces—gables, dark windows, a tower that held on to the sky. One upper window glowed with a dull warmth, a single square that looked almost domestic until you noticed how still it was. Anna took that in and filed it away. Lights had explanations. If she couldn’t find one, she’d write that down too.

A man waited under the portico, coat collar up, posture tight. Mr. Harrowick matched the voice from the phone: careful, controlled, the kind of person who measured his own words as if they cost money. He didn’t offer a dramatic greeting. He offered a keyring and a look that asked her to be competent.

“You’re Anna Karstane,” he said. He pronounced her name correctly, which surprised her.

“That’s me.” She tried a smile that came out more like a baring of teeth. “Student journalist. I’ll do my best to disappoint everyone who wants this to be a séance.”

Harrowick’s mouth twitched, a quick movement he didn’t let become a smile. “I don’t want theatrics,” he said. “I want someone who observes.” His gaze went to the recorder in her hand. “You’re recording?”

“Notes,” Anna said. “Quotes. Details. It keeps me honest.”

He nodded once. “Good. Then here are the terms. You stay inside until dawn. You don’t bring anyone in, and you don’t wander the grounds at night. If you decide to leave, you leave a note on the hall table. Clear and obvious. No riddles.”

“And in the morning?” Anna asked.

“The taxi returns at seven,” Harrowick said. “One knock at the door. If you answer, you leave. If you don’t, the driver waits ten minutes, then goes back to town and calls me. I return with a spare key.” His jaw tightened on the next part. “If I still can’t reach you by nine, I call emergency services. I don’t want attention,” he added, voice flattening, “and I want you alive more.”

Anna raised her brows. “That’s almost reassuring.”

“It’s practical,” Harrowick said. He hesitated, then looked past her shoulder as if the house were standing behind her like a person. “There’s one more thing. If you find… items. Letters, photographs, anything you think is related. Leave them where they are.”

“Because the house doesn’t like it?” Anna asked, aiming for lightness.

Harrowick’s eyes stayed dry. “Because I don’t trust what changes when things are moved,” he said. “And because I’m tired of being wrong about this place.”

The driver cleared his throat. Anna glanced back at the taxi, then at Harrowick. Alone meant alone; her editor had hated that part, but it made the feature cleaner. A witness who could be quoted, a setting that could do its work without an audience.

Harrowick stepped into the taxi’s back seat. The driver pulled away. Headlights swept across the front steps, made the wet stone shine, and then the lane swallowed the light. The engine noise thinned into rain and finally disappeared.

Silence took its place with unsettling speed. Anna stood under the portico and listened. Her pulse felt loud in her ears, and she resented it on principle. She dug out a cigarette, lit it with her back to the wind, and drew smoke into her lungs like it was punctuation.

She exhaled slowly. “It’s just a building,” she told herself, because saying it anchored her. The cigarette burned down faster than she expected. She crushed it under her boot and pocketed the pack again.

The front door opened with a weighty sigh of hinges. Inside, the air smelled of dry wood and something faintly floral, like old sachets. Anna stepped over the threshold and felt the temperature change against her skin, a coolness that didn’t belong to the season.

For a heartbeat, her perception did something odd. The hall seemed to tilt. She had the distinct sensation of hovering an inch behind herself, watching her own shoulders rise and fall under the leather jacket, watching her hand tighten around the keyring. The feeling snapped back so quickly it left a thin nausea behind.

Anna blinked, swallowed, and blamed adrenaline. She’d pulled all-nighters in the newsroom; her body did strange things when it wanted sleep and didn’t get it. That explanation tasted sensible, so she kept it.

The entrance hall rose high enough to swallow sound. A staircase climbed at the far end. Lamps cast weak pools of light through yellowed shades, and portraits covered the walls: severe men, pale women, children dressed like small adults. Their painted eyes didn’t follow her. They didn’t need to.

Anna picked a narrow table in the center of the hall and set up with the kind of deliberate neatness she pretended she didn’t possess. Notebook. Camera. Recorder. Flashlight. She liked order; it made the night feel like a series of tasks rather than a dare.

She pressed the recorder’s button and spoke softly, the way she did when she wanted her future self to trust her past self. “Entry one. Harrowick House. Friday night. Rain. Arrived twenty-two forty-seven.” She paused, listened to the room, then added, almost amused, “And yes, I’m alone. If you’re hearing this and I sound braver than I feel, that’s editing.”

She walked, keeping her dictation going in short bursts—drawing room, covered furniture, a faint smell of dust and fabric; library, rows of books that made her feel like an intruder in someone else’s private mind. She noted the mundane possibilities automatically: drafts, settling beams, animals in walls, old pipes expanding and contracting.

The house offered small interruptions. A cooler patch of air that moved against her wrist when there were no open windows. A subtle shift in pressure near the west corridor that made her ears feel full. Anna caught herself slowing, her body responding before her skepticism could organize a sentence.

She reached the corridor and felt something tighten behind her ribs. The portraits here were closer together, more intimate, less formal. Some of the faces looked unfinished around the edges, as if the painter had been interrupted and never returned.

Halfway down, one painting hung slightly lower than the others, the frame’s angle off by a few degrees. Anna stopped because her eyes stopped. The corridor seemed to narrow toward that frame, the house’s quiet arranging itself into a point.

She had the sense that her next breath mattered. She let it out anyway and stepped closer.

The portrait showed a young woman in nineteenth-century dress—dark lace at the throat, hair pinned in heavy curls. The painter had given her an expression that held both patience and amusement, as if she were waiting for someone to catch up.

Anna’s mouth dried.

The face was hers.

Not in the loose, flattering way people claimed strangers resembled each other. The same cheekbones. The same small asymmetry in the mouth. The same faint shadow beneath the left eye that makeup never fully erased. Even the tiny scar near her hairline, earned in childhood and usually hidden by her bangs, sat there on the painted skin like a signature.

She leaned in until her breath fogged the varnish. A date sat in the corner: **1894**.

Anna stepped back, recorder still running in her pocket, and tried to manufacture an explanation. Prank. Coincidence. Some creepy art student with too much time. Her mind offered options like flashcards, all of them flimsy.

A floorboard creaked upstairs.

Anna froze, then forced herself to move. She walked back to the hall table with the stiff carefulness of someone pretending she isn’t listening. She stopped the recorder, ejected the cassette, and rewound it, the mechanism’s whirr giving her something solid to hold on to.

She pressed play.

At first, the tape gave her rain, her own footsteps, the calmness she’d performed into the microphone. Then came a quiet exhale, so close it raised gooseflesh along her arms.

Anna held the recorder tighter.

A whisper surfaced under the breath, thin and structured, as if the sound had been threaded through static on purpose.

“An…na.”

Her name, pulled apart and put back together by something that didn’t use it often.

The tape clicked. Silence returned.

From the bottom of the staircase, something shifted into the edge of the lamplight—slow, deliberate—claiming the space where her skepticism had been standing a moment before.


## Chapter Two: The House Takes Dictation

Anna stood very still and watched the lamplight by the staircase, waiting for her eyes to decide what they were seeing. The shape at the edge of the glow had moved with intention—she felt that much in her bones—even if her brain wanted to file it under drafts, shadows, and tiredness.

She swallowed and forced herself to breathe like a person doing a job. A reporter didn’t get to panic on schedule. She clicked the recorder off, then on again, and spoke into it in a low voice she hoped sounded steadier than she felt. “Entry two. Playback captured a whisper. It sounded like my name.” She paused, then added, “I’m going to assume this is acoustics until it stops being acoustics.”

The hall held its breath with her.

The shadow by the stairs shifted again—small, slow, as though something had leaned away from the light. Anna’s shoulders tightened. She reached for the flashlight on the table and snapped it on, sweeping the beam across the base of the staircase.

A coat stand stood there, thin and old, its hooks raised like patient hands. No coat. No hat. Just wood worn shiny by a century of fingertips. The lamplight had turned it into a person at the edge of vision, and now, under the flashlight, it looked harmlessly stupid.

Anna let out air through her teeth and felt anger arrive on the heels of relief. “Cute,” she muttered, and hated how the word sounded in the empty house. She circled the stand, checked the floorboards around it, then looked back at the lamp. The shade hung slightly crooked. A hinge somewhere up the stairs complained softly, a single creak like a throat clearing.

She set the flashlight down and rubbed at the corner of her eye. The wet smear of eyeliner came away on her finger, making her look less like a composed cynic and more like a student who’d made a long night personal. She wiped her hand on her jeans and reached for her cigarette pack.

Smoke helped her think in straight lines. She lit up and leaned one hip against the hall table, eyes on the staircase while she inhaled. The lighter’s flame had been the brightest thing in the house since the taxi left. That fact bothered her more than it should have.

She tried the simplest test first: she walked to the front door and checked the lock. Solid. No gap. No draft. She checked the nearest windows. Shut. Latched. The air was still. The temperature felt even, cool but steady.

Anna’s skepticism was a muscle; she’d trained it for years. It had gotten her through professors who loved their own voices and sources who loved a story more than the truth. It worked best when she fed it small tasks.

So she gave herself tasks.

She returned to the staircase, climbed three steps, and stopped. The wood groaned under her weight like an old man waking up. She looked down at the hall from above, at the table where her gear sat in its tidy lineup. Camera. Notebook. Recorder. Flashlight. The little island of control she’d built in a room that didn’t want islands.

A brief dizziness slid through her, the same wrong lightness she’d felt when she first entered. The hall seemed to fall away from her, as though her viewpoint had floated upward and backward, hovering near the ceiling beam. For a second she watched herself from an impossible angle: Anna on the stairs, cigarette ember glowing, hair damp at the ends, one hand gripping the banister as if it could keep her anchored.

Her stomach rolled. She blinked hard and the world snapped back into its usual geometry.

“Stress,” she whispered into the recorder without meaning to, then stopped herself. She didn’t want the tape full of excuses. She wanted it full of facts.

She climbed down again and walked toward the west corridor with the stiff determination of someone refusing to be steered. The portraits watched with their practiced stillness. Their faces belonged to people who had died with secrets in their mouths, and the house had kept those secrets like furniture.

The corridor felt narrower than it had earlier, as though the air itself had thickened. Anna’s skin prickled along her arms. She hated the way her body behaved, offering reactions before thoughts. That belonged to superstition, to the kind of story she’d promised her editor she wouldn’t write.

The portrait waited where she’d left it, hung a fraction too low, frame tilted like a question. Anna kept her distance this time. She took out the camera, advanced the film with a careful click, and took a photo straight-on. The flash fired, harsh and white, collapsing the corridor into a flat image for a heartbeat. When the afterimage faded, the painted woman’s eyes seemed even darker.

Anna wrote in her notebook with a fast hand: *Portrait resembles subject. Date 1894. Photograph taken 23:12.* The act of writing steadied her.

She leaned closer again despite herself, eyes scanning the brushwork for signs of modern tampering. The paint looked old. The cracks in the varnish formed a delicate web across the surface. Her reflection hovered faintly in the gloss: leather jacket, pale face, smudged eyeliner, lips pressed tight.

For a moment, the reflection and the painted face lined up too perfectly, and Anna felt a surge of irritation at her own imagination. She stepped back, breaking the alignment, and the corridor’s temperature seemed to ease by a degree.

Footsteps sounded above her.

Anna turned fast enough to make her necklace bite her throat. The sound came again—one creak, then another—slow and measured. It traveled along the upstairs landing and stopped. Silence followed, thick enough to feel against the ears.

She held the recorder in her palm and kept it rolling without speaking. If this house wanted to perform, she’d let it perform into evidence.

Nothing else moved. No rush of air. No slam of a door. The quiet sat there with an almost polite patience.

Anna returned to the hall and checked the old phone line Harrowick had mentioned. The handset felt heavy, the plastic faintly sticky as if it had absorbed decades of fingerprints. She lifted it to her ear.

No dial tone. Not even a faint hum. Just dead, clean silence.

She put it down carefully and stared at the staircase again. Her cigarette had burned low. She crushed it out and didn’t light another; she refused to let the night turn into a chain of small dependencies.

She sat at the table and rewound the tape she’d recorded earlier. The mechanical whirr made her feel less alone. She pressed play and listened with her head tilted slightly, as if angle could improve reception.

Rain. Her own footsteps. The small rasp of her breath. Then the exhale again—close, intimate, impossible to mistake for a creaking floorboard.

Her fingers tightened around the recorder.

Beneath that breath, the whisper returned with more shape than before. The syllables came gently, spaced as if whoever spoke them was practicing.

“An…na.”

The name sounded familiar and wrong at the same time, like hearing your nickname from someone who never had the right to use it.

Anna stopped the tape and stared at the recorder’s little window, at the spinning reels that had already gone still. She didn’t feel triumphant. She didn’t feel terrified. She felt insulted, as though the house had reached into her pocket and touched something private.

She made herself do the boring checks again because boring checks were how you kept your mind from running off and writing its own headlines. She inspected the recorder’s microphone port for dust. She checked the cassette for defects. She rewound and played the same section twice more.

The whisper held. Every time. Same breath, same soft thread of sound, same careful spacing of her name.

Anna’s skepticism didn’t vanish. It shifted into a new, narrower shape: *How.* If something could be measured, it could be explained, even if the explanation arrived later.

She stood, slid the cassette into her jacket pocket, and picked up the flashlight. She didn’t aim it at the stairs right away. She aimed it at the portraits lining the hall, letting the beam pass over faces and names and frames. Brass plaques glinted. Dates. Titles. A family tree in oil paint.

One frame near the base of the staircase caught the light and threw it back. The glass over the portrait had been recently cleaned, too clean compared to the others. Anna leaned in to read the plaque and found her own reflection staring back at her in the shine.

Behind her reflection, in the dark curve of the staircase, a second figure seemed to stand for half a second—tall, narrow, placed one step higher than she was.

Anna turned, flashlight swinging.

The staircase was empty.

Her heart kicked hard enough to make her dizzy. For an instant the world loosened again, that out-of-step sensation, as if her mind had lifted out of her skull to watch her from above. She clamped a hand on the banister and forced the feeling down through her feet, through the wood, through the only solid thing in reach.

A sound rose from upstairs—soft fabric moving, the kind of noise a person makes when they shift their weight and try to stay quiet.

Anna stared up the staircase, breath shallow, recorder in her pocket like a small captured secret.

Then, from the landing, came a single, deliberate tap—as if someone had touched the railing with a fingernail to make sure she was listening.

parts.title: Prologue
paragraphs: In the autumn of 1894, Harrowick House kept its lights burning later than usual. Rain worried at the windows, and the servants moved through the corridors with lowered voices, carrying coal, lamps, and trays they hoped would not be sent back untouched. Something in the household had shifted during the previous month. No one named it. The house did not encourage that kind of honesty.

The portrait was begun on a Thursday in the west corridor, where the light held longest and the walls kept a little warmth after sunset. The painter, a narrow man with tobacco-stained fingers, had been summoned in haste and told only that the work was to be done quickly, accurately, and without questions. He agreed to two of those terms. By the second sitting he had already decided that accuracy was the dangerous part.

The young woman who posed for him wore black lace at her throat and dark velvet at her wrists. Her posture never faltered. Her expression changed only in degrees too small to measure, yet the room altered whenever she lifted her eyes from her folded hands. On the first day, the painter blamed fatigue for the strange pull of her face. On the second, he blamed the weather. On the third, while mixing shadow into the curve beneath one painted eye, he felt a certainty settle over him with the quiet weight of a hand on the shoulder: he was not painting a likeness for the present, but for someone who had not yet arrived.

When the portrait was finished, the mistress of the house stood before it for a long time without speaking. Then she ordered it hung in the west corridor and instructed the staff that it was never to be removed, never to be covered, and never to be discussed with guests. Years later, after names had faded from ledgers and whole branches of the family had thinned into rumor, the house remained faithful to that instruction. Some promises, once made inside Harrowick House, did not weaken with time. They waited.

parts.title: Chapter One: Byline After Midnight
paragraphs: Anna Karstane told herself she’d agreed to this for the story. That was the clean version she could say out loud: a first-person overnight in a “haunted” estate, written with enough skepticism to annoy the campus occult club and enough atmosphere to make the arts editor smile. The messier truth sat underneath it—her curiosity had teeth, and she’d always had the habit of touching things that warned her away.

The taxi’s heater worked in short bursts, like it was negotiating with the cold. Outside, rain stitched the night into something dense and private. The driver didn’t talk much, which Anna appreciated; she was already drafting the opening paragraph in her head and deleting it at the same time.

She looked like she belonged to the edge of a concert poster rather than a journalism department: black jeans, combat boots, a fitted leather jacket that smelled faintly of smoke and old perfume. A silver choker caught the occasional dashboard glow. Her eyeliner, applied with the confidence of practice, had started to soften at one corner where rain had found its way in—an imperfect smear that made her look tired and defiant at once.

In her bag were the tools of a student reporter from the late eighties: a notepad with dog-eared pages, a cheap film camera she’d borrowed from the media lab, and a small cassette recorder she used to dictate quotes so she wouldn’t lose them to adrenaline. She didn’t call it ghost-hunting. She called it documentation.

Her editor at the student paper had made a show of being responsible. *Don’t sensationalize,* he’d said, pushing his glasses up. *And don’t sue us.* Then he’d leaned closer and added, *If you come back with something good, I’ll put it above the sports column for once.*

Harrowick House sat on the edge of a town that pretended it didn’t have a history. The older locals didn’t talk about the manor so much as they talked around it, the way people skirted potholes they’d hit before. A cracked sign leaned in ivy: HARROWICK HOUSE. The taxi turned onto the gravel lane, and the tires made a sound like someone quietly grinding their teeth.

The estate appeared through the rain in pieces—gables, dark windows, a tower that held on to the sky. One upper window glowed with a dull warmth, a single square that looked almost domestic until you noticed how still it was. Anna took that in and filed it away. Lights had explanations. If she couldn’t find one, she’d write that down too.

A man waited under the portico, coat collar up, posture tight. Mr. Harrowick matched the voice from the phone: careful, controlled, the kind of person who measured his own words as if they cost money. He didn’t offer a dramatic greeting. He offered a keyring and a look that asked her to be competent.
“You’re Anna Karstane,” he said. He pronounced her name correctly, which surprised her.
“That’s me.” She tried a smile that came out more like a baring of teeth. “Student journalist. I’ll do my best to disappoint everyone who wants this to be a séance.”

Harrowick’s mouth twitched, a quick movement he didn’t let become a smile. “I don’t want theatrics,” he said. “I want someone who observes.” His gaze went to the recorder in her hand. “You’re recording?”
“Notes,” Anna said. “Quotes. Details. It keeps me honest.”

He nodded once. “Good. Then here are the terms. You stay inside until dawn. You don’t bring anyone in, and you don’t wander the grounds at night. If you decide to leave, you leave a note on the hall table. Clear and obvious. No riddles.”
“And in the morning?” Anna asked.

“The taxi returns at seven,” Harrowick said. “One knock at the door. If you answer, you leave. If you don’t, the driver waits ten minutes, then goes back to town and calls me. I return with a spare key.” His jaw tightened on the next part. “If I still can’t reach you by nine, I call emergency services. I don’t want attention,” he added, voice flattening, “and I want you alive more.”
Anna raised her brows. “That’s almost reassuring.”

“It’s practical,” Harrowick said. He hesitated, then looked past her shoulder as if the house were standing behind her like a person. “There’s one more thing. If you find… items. Letters, photographs, anything you think is related. Leave them where they are.”
“Because the house doesn’t like it?” Anna asked, aiming for lightness.
Harrowick’s eyes stayed dry. “Because I don’t trust what changes when things are moved,” he said. “And because I’m tired of being wrong about this place.”

The driver cleared his throat. Anna glanced back at the taxi, then at Harrowick. Alone meant alone; her editor had hated that part, but it made the feature cleaner. A witness who could be quoted, a setting that could do its work without an audience.

Harrowick stepped into the taxi’s back seat. The driver pulled away. Headlights swept across the front steps, made the wet stone shine, and then the lane swallowed the light. The engine noise thinned into rain and finally disappeared.

Silence took its place with unsettling speed. Anna stood under the portico and listened. Her pulse felt loud in her ears, and she resented it on principle. She dug out a cigarette, lit it with her back to the wind, and drew smoke into her lungs like it was punctuation.

She exhaled slowly. “It’s just a building,” she told herself, because saying it anchored her. The cigarette burned down faster than she expected. She crushed it under her boot and pocketed the pack again.

The front door opened with a weighty sigh of hinges. Inside, the air smelled of dry wood and something faintly floral, like old sachets. Anna stepped over the threshold and felt the temperature change against her skin, a coolness that didn’t belong to the season.

For a heartbeat, her perception did something odd. The hall seemed to tilt. She had the distinct sensation of hovering an inch behind herself, watching her own shoulders rise and fall under the leather jacket, watching her hand tighten around the keyring. The feeling snapped back so quickly it left a thin nausea behind.

Anna blinked, swallowed, and blamed adrenaline. She’d pulled all-nighters in the newsroom; her body did strange things when it wanted sleep and didn’t get it. That explanation tasted sensible, so she kept it.

The entrance hall rose high enough to swallow sound. A staircase climbed at the far end. Lamps cast weak pools of light through yellowed shades, and portraits covered the walls: severe men, pale women, children dressed like small adults. Their painted eyes didn’t follow her. They didn’t need to.

Anna picked a narrow table in the center of the hall and set up with the kind of deliberate neatness she pretended she didn’t possess. Notebook. Camera. Recorder. Flashlight. She liked order; it made the night feel like a series of tasks rather than a dare.

She pressed the recorder’s button and spoke softly, the way she did when she wanted her future self to trust her past self. “Entry one. Harrowick House. Friday night. Rain. Arrived twenty-two forty-seven.” She paused, listened to the room, then added, almost amused, “And yes, I’m alone. If you’re hearing this and I sound braver than I feel, that’s editing.”

She walked, keeping her dictation going in short bursts—drawing room, covered furniture, a faint smell of dust and fabric; library, rows of books that made her feel like an intruder in someone else’s private mind. She noted the mundane possibilities automatically: drafts, settling beams, animals in walls, old pipes expanding and contracting.

The house offered small interruptions. A cooler patch of air that moved against her wrist when there were no open windows. A subtle shift in pressure near the west corridor that made her ears feel full. Anna caught herself slowing, her body responding before her skepticism could organize a sentence.

She reached the corridor and felt something tighten behind her ribs. The portraits here were closer together, more intimate, less formal. Some of the faces looked unfinished around the edges, as if the painter had been interrupted and never returned.

Halfway down, one painting hung slightly lower than the others, the frame’s angle off by a few degrees. Anna stopped because her eyes stopped. The corridor seemed to narrow toward that frame, the house’s quiet arranging itself into a point.
She had the sense that her next breath mattered. She let it out anyway and stepped closer.

The portrait showed a young woman in nineteenth-century dress—dark lace at the throat, hair pinned in heavy curls. The painter had given her an expression that held both patience and amusement, as if she were waiting for someone to catch up.
Anna’s mouth dried.
The face was hers.

Not in the loose, flattering way people claimed strangers resembled each other. The same cheekbones. The same small asymmetry in the mouth. The same faint shadow beneath the left eye that makeup never fully erased. Even the tiny scar near her hairline, earned in childhood and usually hidden by her bangs, sat there on the painted skin like a signature.
She leaned in until her breath fogged the varnish. A date sat in the corner: **1894**.

Anna stepped back, recorder still running in her pocket, and tried to manufacture an explanation. Prank. Coincidence. Some creepy art student with too much time. Her mind offered options like flashcards, all of them flimsy.
A floorboard creaked upstairs.

Anna froze, then forced herself to move. She walked back to the hall table with the stiff carefulness of someone pretending she isn’t listening. She stopped the recorder, ejected the cassette, and rewound it, the mechanism’s whirr giving her something solid to hold on to.
She pressed play.
At first, the tape gave her rain, her own footsteps, the calmness she’d performed into the microphone. Then came a quiet exhale, so close it raised gooseflesh along her arms.
Anna held the recorder tighter.
A whisper surfaced under the breath, thin and structured, as if the sound had been threaded through static on purpose.
“An…na.”
Her name, pulled apart and put back together by something that didn’t use it often.
The tape clicked. Silence returned.
From the bottom of the staircase, something shifted into the edge of the lamplight—slow, deliberate—claiming the space where her skepticism had been standing a moment before.

chapter.summary: In the prologue, a mysterious portrait painted in 1894 hangs in Harrowick House’s west corridor, commissioned for a woman who seems to anticipate a future viewer; the house’s matriarch orders it never to be moved or spoken of. Years later, skeptical student journalist Anna Karstane accepts an overnight assignment to document the “haunted” estate, packing a notebook, camera and cassette recorder. After a terse meeting with the austere Mr. Harrowick, who gives her strict rules—stay inside until dawn, leave any found items where they are, and record any observations—Anna settles in the foyer, noting the house’s creaking, shifting air and the portraits lining the walls. In the west corridor she discovers a painting that is an uncanny likeness of herself, complete with a childhood scar and the date 1894, prompting a brief panic‑filled search for a rational explanation. While replaying her recorder, she hears a faint, distorted whisper of her own name, “An‑na,” and then sees a vague shape emerge from the lamplight at the bottom of the staircase, hinting that the house’s unseen presence has finally taken notice of her.

She didn’t call it ghost-hunting. She called it documentation.

Her editor at the student paper had made a show of being responsible. *Don’t sensationalize,* he’d said, pushing his glasses up. *And don’t sue us.* Then he’d leaned closer and added, *If you come back with something good, I’ll put it above the sports column for once.*

Harrowick House sat on the edge of a town that pretended it didn’t have a history. The older locals didn’t talk about the manor so much as they talked around it, the way people skirted potholes they’d hit before. A cracked sign leaned in ivy: HARROWICK HOUSE. The taxi turned onto the gravel lane, and the tires made a sound like someone quietly grinding their teeth.

The estate appeared through the rain in pieces—gables, dark windows, a tower that held on to the sky. One upper window glowed with a dull warmth, a single square that looked almost domestic until you noticed how still it was. Anna took that in and filed it away. Lights had explanations. If she couldn’t find one, she’d write that down too.

A man waited under the portico, coat collar up, posture tight. Mr. Harrowick matched the voice from the phone: careful, controlled, the kind of person who measured his own words as if they cost money. He didn’t offer a dramatic greeting. He offered a keyring and a look that asked her to be competent.

“You’re Anna Karstane,” he said. He pronounced her name correctly, which surprised her.

“That’s me.” She tried a smile that came out more like a baring of teeth. “Student journalist. I’ll do my best to disappoint everyone who wants this to be a séance.”

Harrowick’s mouth twitched, a quick movement he didn’t let become a smile. “I don’t want theatrics,” he said. “I want someone who observes.” His gaze went to the recorder in her hand. “You’re recording?”

“Notes,” Anna said. “Quotes. Details. It keeps me honest.”

He nodded once. “Good. Then here are the terms. You stay inside until dawn. You don’t bring anyone in, and you don’t wander the grounds at night. If you decide to leave, you leave a note on the hall table. Clear and obvious. No riddles.”

“And in the morning?” Anna asked.

“The taxi returns at seven,” Harrowick said. “One knock at the door. If you answer, you leave. If you don’t, the driver waits ten minutes, then goes back to town and calls me. I return with a spare key.” His jaw tightened on the next part. “If I still can’t reach you by nine, I call emergency services. I don’t want attention,” he added, voice flattening, “and I want you alive more.”

Anna raised her brows. “That’s almost reassuring.”

“It’s practical,” Harrowick said. He hesitated, then looked past her shoulder as if the house were standing behind her like a person. “There’s one more thing. If you find… items. Letters, photographs, anything you think is related. Leave them where they are.”

“Because the house doesn’t like it?” Anna asked, aiming for lightness.

Harrowick’s eyes stayed dry. “Because I don’t trust what changes when things are moved,” he said. “And because I’m tired of being wrong about this place.”

The driver cleared his throat. Anna glanced back at the taxi, then at Harrowick. Alone meant alone; her editor had hated that part, but it made the feature cleaner. A witness who could be quoted, a setting that could do its work without an audience.

Harrowick stepped into the taxi’s back seat. The driver pulled away. Headlights swept across the front steps, made the wet stone shine, and then the lane swallowed the light. The engine noise thinned into rain and finally disappeared.

Silence took its place with unsettling speed. Anna stood under the portico and listened. Her pulse felt loud in her ears, and she resented it on principle. She dug out a cigarette, lit it with her back to the wind, and drew smoke into her lungs like it was punctuation.

She exhaled slowly. “It’s just a building,” she told herself, because saying it anchored her. The cigarette burned down faster than she expected. She crushed it under her boot and pocketed the pack again.

The front door opened with a weighty sigh of hinges. Inside, the air smelled of dry wood and something faintly floral, like old sachets. Anna stepped over the threshold and felt the temperature change against her skin, a coolness that didn’t belong to the season.

For a heartbeat, her perception did something odd. The hall seemed to tilt. She had the distinct sensation of hovering an inch behind herself, watching her own shoulders rise and fall under the leather jacket, watching her hand tighten around the keyring. The feeling snapped back so quickly it left a thin nausea behind.

Anna blinked, swallowed, and blamed adrenaline. She’d pulled all-nighters in the newsroom; her body did strange things when it wanted sleep and didn’t get it. That explanation tasted sensible, so she kept it.

The entrance hall rose high enough to swallow sound. A staircase climbed at the far end. Lamps cast weak pools of light through yellowed shades, and portraits covered the walls: severe men, pale women, children dressed like small adults. Their painted eyes didn’t follow her. They didn’t need to.

Anna picked a narrow table in the center of the hall and set up with the kind of deliberate neatness she pretended she didn’t possess. Notebook. Camera. Recorder. Flashlight. She liked order; it made the night feel like a series of tasks rather than a dare.

She pressed the recorder’s button and spoke softly, the way she did when she wanted her future self to trust her past self. “Entry one. Harrowick House. Friday night. Rain. Arrived twenty-two forty-seven.” She paused, listened to the room, then added, almost amused, “And yes, I’m alone. If you’re hearing this and I sound braver than I feel, that’s editing.”

She walked, keeping her dictation going in short bursts—drawing room, covered furniture, a faint smell of dust and fabric; library, rows of books that made her feel like an intruder in someone else’s private mind. She noted the mundane possibilities automatically: drafts, settling beams, animals in walls, old pipes expanding and contracting.

The house offered small interruptions. A cooler patch of air that moved against her wrist when there were no open windows. A subtle shift in pressure near the west corridor that made her ears feel full. Anna caught herself slowing, her body responding before her skepticism could organize a sentence.

She reached the corridor and felt something tighten behind her ribs. The portraits here were closer together, more intimate, less formal. Some of the faces looked unfinished around the edges, as if the painter had been interrupted and never returned.

Halfway down, one painting hung slightly lower than the others, the frame’s angle off by a few degrees. Anna stopped because her eyes stopped. The corridor seemed to narrow toward that frame, the house’s quiet arranging itself into a point.

She had the sense that her next breath mattered. She let it out anyway and stepped closer.

The portrait showed a young woman in nineteenth-century dress—dark lace at the throat, hair pinned in heavy curls. The painter had given her an expression that held both patience and amusement, as if she were waiting for someone to catch up.

Anna’s mouth dried.

The face was hers.

Not in the loose, flattering way people claimed strangers resembled each other. The same cheekbones. The same small asymmetry in the mouth. The same faint shadow beneath the left eye that makeup never fully erased. Even the tiny scar near her hairline, earned in childhood and usually hidden by her bangs, sat there on the painted skin like a signature.

She leaned in until her breath fogged the varnish. A date sat in the corner: **1894**.

Anna stepped back, recorder still running in her pocket, and tried to manufacture an explanation. Prank. Coincidence. Some creepy art student with too much time. Her mind offered options like flashcards, all of them flimsy.

A floorboard creaked upstairs.

Anna froze, then forced herself to move. She walked back to the hall table with the stiff carefulness of someone pretending she isn’t listening. She stopped the recorder, ejected the cassette, and rewound it, the mechanism’s whirr giving her something solid to hold on to.

She pressed play.

At first, the tape gave her rain, her own footsteps, the calmness she’d performed into the microphone. Then came a quiet exhale, so close it raised gooseflesh along her arms.

Anna held the recorder tighter.

A whisper surfaced under the breath, thin and structured, as if the sound had been threaded through static on purpose.

“An…na.”

Her name, pulled apart and put back together by something that didn’t use it often.

The tape clicked. Silence returned.

From the bottom of the staircase, something shifted into the edge of the lamplight—slow, deliberate—claiming the space where her skepticism had been standing a moment before.

Not in the loose, flattering way people claimed strangers resembled each other. The same cheekbones. The same small asymmetry in the mouth. The same faint shadow beneath the left eye that makeup never fully erased. Even the tiny scar near her hairline, earned in childhood and usually hidden by her bangs, sat there on the painted skin like a signature.

### Scene 15 Recognition

She leaned in until her breath fogged the varnish. A date sat in the corner: **1894**.

Anna stepped back, recorder still running in her pocket, and tried to manufacture an explanation. Prank. Coincidence. Some creepy art student with too much time. Her mind offered options like flashcards, all of them flimsy.

### Scene 16 Recorded whisper

A floorboard creaked upstairs.

Anna froze, then forced herself to move. She walked back to the hall table with the stiff carefulness of someone pretending she isn’t listening. She stopped the recorder, ejected the cassette, and rewound it, the mechanism’s whirr giving her something solid to hold on to.

She pressed play.

At first, the tape gave her rain, her own footsteps, the calmness she’d performed into the microphone. Then came a quiet exhale, so close it raised gooseflesh along her arms.

Anna held the recorder tighter.

A whisper surfaced under the breath, thin and structured, as if the sound had been threaded through static on purpose.

“An…na.”

### Scene 17 Emerging presence

Her name, pulled apart and put back together by something that didn’t use it often.

The tape clicked. Silence returned.

From the bottom of the staircase, something shifted into the edge of the lamplight—slow, deliberate—claiming the space where her skepticism had been standing a moment before.
